vulnerabilities.description: Raw ERC20 transfer/transferFrom call without SafeERC20
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 which handles non-standard tokens (USDT etc. don't return a bool). Replace token.transfer(to, amount) with SafeERC20.safeTransfer(token, to, amount).

vulnerabilities.description: Raw ERC20 transfer/transferFrom call without SafeERC20
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 which handles non-standard tokens (USDT etc. don't return a bool). Replace token.transfer(to, amount) with SafeERC20.safeTransfer(token, to, amount).

vulnerabilities.description: Single-step ownership transfer used instead of two-step pattern
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's Ownable2Step contract instead of plain Ownable. This requires the new owner to accept ownership via acceptOwnership(), preventing accidental loss.
Check if the token has transfer restriction functions that could trap sellers. A honeypot typically allows buys but not sells.

vulnerabilities.snippet:
let result := delegatecall(gas, implementation, 0, calldatasize, 0, 0)

                                 
      returndatacopy(0, 0, returndatasize)

      switch result
                                          
      cas
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Review delegatecall usage carefully. Ensure the target is from a trusted source and properly validated.
Add appropriate access control to all administrative functions. Use OpenZeppelin's AccessControl or Ownable for role-based permissions.

vulnerabilities.description: Cross-contract call chain with shared state
vulnerabilities.snippet: pragma solidity ^0.4.24;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use reentrancy guards across all public/external functions in interconnected contracts. Consider using OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard in all contracts in the system.

vulnerabilities.description: Potential function selector collision (requires AST analysis)
vulnerabilities.snippet: pragma solidity ^0.4.24;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use a proxy with an explicit function registry or use transparent proxy pattern that prevents function selector clashes.
Never use delegatecall with user-controlled addresses. Whitelist trusted library addresses. Consider using static libraries instead.

vulnerabilities.description: Function with delegatecall and user-controlled target
vulnerabilities.recommendation: If delegatecall is necessary, restrict target to a whitelist of approved contracts.

vulnerabilities.description: Raw arithmetic in Solidity <0.8 (no built-in overflow check)
vulnerabilities.snippet: pragma solidity 0.4.24;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Upgrade to Solidity 0.8.0+ for built-in overflow checking, or use OpenZeppelin's SafeMath library for older versions.

vulnerabilities.description: renounceOwnership in code (permanently locks owner functions)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Before renouncing ownership, consider: 1. Adding a timelock so changes can be reviewed 2. Setting up a multisig as the new owner 3. Ensuring no critical functions depend on owner access Once renounced, there is NO recovery.

vulnerabilities.description: renounceOwnership in code (permanently locks owner functions)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Before renouncing ownership, consider: 1. Adding a timelock so changes can be reviewed 2. Setting up a multisig as the new owner 3. Ensuring no critical functions depend on owner access Once renounced, there is NO recovery.

vulnerabilities.description: Single-step ownership transfer used instead of two-step pattern
vulnerabilities.location:
function transferOwnership(address newOwner) public onlyOwner {
    pendingOwner = newOwner;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's Ownable2Step contract instead of plain Ownable. This requires the new owner to accept ownership via acceptOwnership(), preventing accidental loss.
Add nonReentrant modifier to functions using _safeMint, _safeTransfer, or safeTransferFrom. Ensure all state changes happen BEFORE the callback.

vulnerabilities.description: Raw arithmetic in Solidity <0.8 (no built-in overflow check)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Upgrade to Solidity 0.8.0+ for built-in overflow checking, or use OpenZeppelin's SafeMath library for older versions.

vulnerabilities.description: Raw ERC20 transfer/transferFrom call without SafeERC20
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 which handles non-standard tokens (USDT etc. don't return a bool). Replace token.transfer(to, amount) with SafeERC20.safeTransfer(token, to, amount).

vulnerabilities.description: Functions with whenPaused but no unpause function
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Add an unpause function protected by onlyOwner. Both pause and unpause should exist as a pair.

vulnerabilities.description: Functions with whenPaused but no unpause function
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Add an unpause function protected by onlyOwner. Both pause and unpause should exist as a pair.

vulnerabilities.description: Mint function without onlyOwner or equivalent access control
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Add access control modifier (onlyOwner, onlyRole, etc.) to the mint function. Consider adding a maximum supply cap and rate limiting.

vulnerabilities.location:
ecrecover(digest, v, r, s);
        require(signatory != address(0)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Always include a nonce (incremented per user) and chainId in your EIP-712 typed data to prevent replay attacks across chains and repeated use.

vulnerabilities.location:
ecrecover(digest, v, r, s);
        require(signatory != address(0)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Always include a nonce (incremented per user) and chainId in your EIP-712 typed data to prevent replay attacks across chains and repeated use.
Use .call{value: amount}('') instead of .send() or .transfer(). The send/transfer 2300-gas pattern can cause DoS for contract recipients.
Always check the return value of call():  (bool success, ) = addr.call{value: amount}(""); require(success, "Transfer failed");
Always check the return value of call():  (bool success, ) = addr.call{value: amount}(""); require(success, "Transfer failed");
Review delegatecall usage carefully. Ensure the target is from a trusted source and properly validated.
Review delegatecall usage carefully. Ensure the target is from a trusted source and properly validated.
Review delegatecall usage carefully. Ensure the target is from a trusted source and properly validated.
Arbitrary delegatecall with user data is EXTREMELY dangerous. This gives the caller full control of the contract's state. Remove this pattern immediately.
Add appropriate access control to all administrative functions. Use OpenZeppelin's AccessControl or Ownable for role-based permissions.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple functions modifying shared state with external calls
vulnerabilities.snippet: pragma solidity ^0.6.0;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard on ALL functions that modify state and make external calls. Alternatively, follow the checks-effects-interactions pattern strictly.

vulnerabilities.description: Cross-contract call chain with shared state
vulnerabilities.snippet: pragma solidity ^0.6.0;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use reentrancy guards across all public/external functions in interconnected contracts. Consider using OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard in all contracts in the system.

vulnerabilities.snippet: pragma solidity ^0.6.0;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use a proxy with an explicit function registry or use transparent proxy pattern that prevents function selector clashes.

vulnerabilities.description: grantRole or revokeRole without proper access control
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Ensure grantRole and revokeRole are protected by the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE role or equivalent access control. Use OpenZeppelin's AccessControlDefaultAdminRules for production.

vulnerabilities.description: grantRole or revokeRole without proper access control
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Ensure grantRole and revokeRole are protected by the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE role or equivalent access control. Use OpenZeppelin's AccessControlDefaultAdminRules for production.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple functions modifying shared state with external calls
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard on ALL functions that modify state and make external calls. Alternatively, follow the checks-effects-interactions pattern strictly.

vulnerabilities.description: Upgradeable contract declares a constructor (state set in a constructor is not applied to proxy instances)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: In upgradeable/proxy contracts, move state initialization into an initialize() function guarded by the 'initializer' modifier. Keep the constructor empty (or use it only for immutables and to lock the implementation via _disableInitializers()).
Check extcodesize(target) before low-level calls, or rely on high-level Solidity function calls that revert when target has no code.

vulnerabilities.description: Missing initializer modifier on init function
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Add the initializer modifier (from OpenZeppelin) to prevent re-initialization. Consider using Initializable.sol or UUPSUpgradeable pattern with constructor-based initialization.

vulnerabilities.description: Initialize function without access control modifiers
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Add the 'initializer' modifier (from OpenZeppelin) or 'onlyOwner' to the initialize function. Also add a check that initialization can only happen once.

vulnerabilities.description: Upgradeable contract declares a constructor (state set in a constructor is not applied to proxy instances)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: In upgradeable/proxy contracts, move state initialization into an initialize() function guarded by the 'initializer' modifier. Keep the constructor empty (or use it only for immutables and to lock the implementation via _disableInitializers()).

vulnerabilities.description: Raw ERC20 transfer/transferFrom call without SafeERC20
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 which handles non-standard tokens (USDT etc. don't return a bool). Replace token.transfer(to, amount) with SafeERC20.safeTransfer(token, to, amount).

vulnerabilities.description: Function with delegatecall and user-controlled target
vulnerabilities.recommendation: If delegatecall is necessary, restrict target to a whitelist of approved contracts.
Add appropriate access control to all administrative functions. Use OpenZeppelin's AccessControl or Ownable for role-based permissions.
Use .call{value: amount}('') instead of .send() or .transfer(). The send/transfer 2300-gas pattern can cause DoS for contract recipients.

vulnerabilities.description: Raw arithmetic in Solidity <0.8 (no built-in overflow check)
vulnerabilities.snippet: pragma solidity 0.6.12;
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Upgrade to Solidity 0.8.0+ for built-in overflow checking, or use OpenZeppelin's SafeMath library for older versions.

Always check the return value of call():  (bool success, ) = addr.call{value: amount}(""); require(success, "Transfer failed");

vulnerabilities.snippet:
let result := delegatecall(
                gas(),
                implementation,
                0,
                calldatasize(),
                0,
                0
            )
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Review delegatecall usage carefully. Ensure the target is from a trusted source and properly validated.

Never make external calls to user-supplied addresses without a whitelist. If arbitrary calls are needed, implement: 1) A whitelist of approved target addresses 2) Function signature filtering 3) Rate limiting and value caps
Add appropriate access control to all administrative functions. Use OpenZeppelin's AccessControl or Ownable for role-based permissions.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple functions modifying shared state with external calls
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard on ALL functions that modify state and make external calls. Alternatively, follow the checks-effects-interactions pattern strictly.

vulnerabilities.description: Cross-contract call chain with shared state
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use reentrancy guards across all public/external functions in interconnected contracts. Consider using OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard in all contracts in the system.

vulnerabilities.description: Potential function selector collision (requires AST analysis)
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use a proxy with an explicit function registry or use transparent proxy pattern that prevents function selector clashes.
Check extcodesize(target) before low-level calls, or rely on high-level Solidity function calls that revert when target has no code.

vulnerabilities.snippet:
let result := delegatecall(
                gas(),
                implementation,
                0,
                calldatasize(),
                0,
                0
            )

                                       
            ret
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Never use delegatecall with user-controlled addresses. Whitelist trusted library addresses. Consider using static libraries instead.

vulnerabilities.description: Function with delegatecall and user-controlled target
vulnerabilities.recommendation: If delegatecall is necessary, restrict target to a whitelist of approved contracts.

vulnerabilities.description: Mint function without onlyOwner or equivalent access control
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Add access control modifier (onlyOwner, onlyRole, etc.) to the mint function. Consider adding a maximum supply cap and rate limiting.
Replace tx.origin with msg.sender for access control. tx.origin should never be used for authorization — use msg.sender which is the direct caller.

vulnerabilities.description: Mint function without onlyOwner or equivalent access control

vulnerabilities.description: Raw ERC20 transfer/transferFrom call without SafeERC20
vulnerabilities.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 which handles non-standard tokens (USDT etc. don't return a bool). Replace token.transfer(to, amount) with SafeERC20.safeTransfer(token, to, amount).
